text: This video will demonstrate a robotic retrorectus ETEP chevron incisional hernia repair with right-sided TAR. This is a patient with diabetes and a BMI of thirty-six who has a nine by nineteen centimeter incisional hernia at his chevron incision. As you can see, the hernia extends from the right linea semilinearis to just past the midline slightly into the left rectus muscle.

The surgeon uses the XI robot with three ports placed along the left linea semilunaris. The two lower ports are shifted medially to access the right upper quadrant and the ipsilateral upper left retrorectus space, addressing the hernia measuring nine centimeters vertically and nineteen centimeters transversely.
The surgeon places a large retromuscular mesh with significant overlap, performing a transversus abdominis release (TAR) on the right side to secure the mesh laterally beyond the hernia defect.

After docking the robot, the surgeon opens the posterior rectus sheath on the patient's left side near the linea alba, entering the preperitoneal space behind the diastatic linea alba. The surgeon works cephalad, revealing scarring of the rectus muscle against the posterior sheath and attempts to identify the linea alba while separating the rectus muscle from the posterior rectus sheath.

The surgeon is in the intraperitoneal position, working to take down adhesions or existing peritoneum near the xiphoid. The dissection continues at the cephalad portion of the chevron incision in the midline, cutting the posterior rectus sheath. The ETEP technique is used for accessing and maneuvering within the abdominal cavity during the complex incisional hernia repair.

The surgeon enters the right retrorectus space above the chevron incision, using a grasper to identify and follow the linea alba. The main incisional hernia is exposed, and the surgeon carefully reduces the pseudosac to ensure no bowel is present, revealing the medial edge of the defect. The hernia contains mostly omentum, which is easily taken down, completing the pseudosac reduction on the medial side of the defect.

The surgeon navigates the abdominal cavity to visualize the contents of the hernia at the right lateral corner of the chevron scar. The dissection proceeds in the retrorectus position, revealing minimal remaining rectus muscle near the linea semilunaris.

The surgeon dissects the retro-rectus space lateral to the chevron scar and begins the transversus abdominis release at the xiphoid area. The dissection proceeds behind the xiphoid, identifying a natural tissue plane in the fatty layer, followed by cutting the posterior rectus sheath to initiate the transversus abdominis release.

The surgeon carefully dissects under the diaphragm, ensuring no diaphragm fibers are brought down, while preparing to tuck the mesh beneath it. The dissection extends approximately four to five centimeters under the diaphragm, continuing the top-down transversus abdominis release (TAR).

The surgeon begins by incising the posterior lamella of the internal oblique muscle, using one side of the scissors to shimmy behind the muscle and lift it. Cautery and scissors are employed to cut the transversus abdominis muscle, transitioning from the diaphragm to the transversus abdominis. The dissection stays close to the muscle to maintain a pre-transverse salus position in the upper abdomen.

The surgeon navigates a thick layer of transverse salus fascia, carefully avoiding perforation of the perineum. Despite scarring and minimal posterior sheath near the linea semilunaris, a clear plane is identified between the transverse abdominis and transverse salus fascia. Scar tissue from a previous drain is visible, and the transition from the pre-transverse salis plane to the pre-peritoneal plane is observed.

The surgeon takes down the peritoneum, completing the caudal dissection of the transversus abdominis release (TAR). An extensive pocket is visible, which will accommodate a significant overlap with the mesh.

The surgeon measures the hernia defect, which spans nine centimeters in the cranial-caudal direction and nineteen centimeters transversely. Multiple V-lock sutures are laid out across the defect without tightening, resembling a corset when tension is applied. The defect involves the diastatic linea alba but does not extend to the left side. The defect is re-approximated transversely, and the posterior layer is closed to prevent bowel from entering the retro-rectus space.

The surgeon closes the remaining material, including peritoneum, posterior sheath, or pseudosac, to patch large defects. A piece of medium weight macroporous polypropylene mesh is cut to a 30 by 30 centimeter size with rounded corners, providing a good overlap of the defect.
The surgeon places a 19 French round JP drain into the large surgical space, noting the redundancy of the medium weight macroporous mesh, which shows slight wrinkling and overlap.
